task: I’ve got a subcontractor dispute brewing over who’s on the hook for a damaged section, and I want to handle it the right way before it turns into a bigger headache. What should I gather and put in writing first so I’m not boxed in later?

turns.agent_response:
Before you say much to either side, lock down the facts in writing so nobody can rewrite the story later.

### Gather first
- **Photos/video** of the damaged section from multiple angles
- **Date/time** the damage was found and when it likely happened
- **Exact location** on the job
- **Who was working there** before, during, and after
- **Daily reports / foreman logs**
- **Delivery tickets, pour logs, inspection notes, RFIs, emails, texts**
- **Scope sheets / subcontracts** showing who owned that work
- **Any witness names** who saw the condition before or after
- **Weather or site conditions** if they matter
- **Your own timeline** of what happened, in order

### Put this in writing right away
Send a short written notice to the involved subs and GC/owner if needed:

- the **damaged area**
- the **date it was discovered**
- that it is being **documented and preserved**
- that **no one is to repair, cover, or disturb it** until it’s reviewed
- that responsibility is **not being assigned yet** pending review
- that you want each party to **state their position in writing**

### Best practice
Use a simple “freeze the issue” approach:
- **Document it**
- **Notify everyone who may be involved**
- **Hold off on repairs**
- **Get each party’s version in writing**
- **Confirm who has authority to direct the fix**

### Keep it factual
Don’t argue fault in the first note. Just state:
- what was found
- when
- where
- who was present
- that it needs review before any corrective work

### Simple wording you can use
> We found damage at [location] on [date/time]. The area is being documented and should not be repaired, covered, or disturbed until reviewed. Please preserve all related records and send your written position on how this occurred and who you believe is responsible.

If you want, I can draft a tighter **notice to subcontractors** or a **damage hold notice** you can send today.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. But I want it tighter before anybody starts pointing fingers.

Need these in the file too:
- **contract language** on damage, supervision, and cleanup
- **change orders / directives** tied to that area
- **who had control of the work zone** that day
- **photos before the damage**, if I’ve got them
- **equipment logs** if a machine was involved
- **names of the actual crew on site**, not just the company
- **any notice deadlines** in the subcontract

And the first written notice should say one more thing: **preserve all evidence, including phone photos, messages, and video**. People “lose” stuff fast when money’s on the line.

If you want to be useful, draft me a short hold-notice and a separate version that’s a little firmer for the sub who likely caused it.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely. Here are two tight versions you can use.

## 1) Neutral hold-notice
**Subject:** Preservation Notice / Damaged Area at [Project / Location]

We identified damage at **[exact location]** on **[date/time discovered]**. This notice is to document the condition and preserve all related information pending review.

Please **preserve all evidence** connected to this issue, including but not limited to:
- photos and video
- phone photos/messages
- text messages
- emails
- daily reports
- crew logs
- equipment logs
- delivery tickets
- inspection records
- any other related documents or communications

Do **not repair, cover, remove, disturb, or alter** the affected area until it has been reviewed and cleared.

Please also send, in writing:
- who had control of the work zone that day
- who was present on site
- what work was ongoing at the time
- any directives, approvals, or change orders tied to the area
- your position on how the condition occurred

This notice is for preservation and documentation only and is **not an assignment of fault**.

---

## 2) Firmer version for the likely responsible sub
**Subject:** Immediate Preservation Notice / Damaged Work at [Project / Location]

We have identified damage at **[exact location]** on **[date/time discovered]**. Based on the current information, your work appears to be involved, so this notice is to require immediate preservation of evidence and a written response.

You must **preserve all evidence** related to this issue, including:
- all photos and video
- phone images and messages
- text messages
- emails
- daily reports
- crew names
- equipment logs
- delivery tickets
- inspection records
- directives, approvals, and change orders
- any other related records or communications

Do **not repair, cover, disturb, or dispose of** anything related to the affected area until further notice.

Also provide, in writing:
- the names of the actual crew members on site
- who supervised the work
- who had control of the work zone
- what work was being performed at the time
- any instruction or approval you received
- your explanation of how the damage occurred

This notice is being issued to preserve the record and protect all parties while responsibility is reviewed.

---

## What I’d make sure is in your file right now
For your dispute package, keep it tight and factual:
- photos/video of the damage
- photos **before** the damage if you have them
- date/time discovered
- exact location
- what work was underway
- who had control of the area
- actual crew names, not just company name
- supervisor/foreman names
- contract language on damage, supervision, cleanup
- change orders/directives tied to that area
- equipment logs if machinery was involved
- notice deadlines in the subcontract
- witness names and phone numbers
- who was informed: foreman, superintendent, inspector, owner rep

If you want, I can also turn one of these into a **more formal email** or a **harder construction notice** with stronger contract-style language.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s usable.

The firmer one is the one I’d send first if I already know who’s likely on the hook. Keep it short, no speeches. I’d add one line at the bottom: **failure to preserve evidence may be treated as spoliation**. That usually gets attention.

The file list is right. I’d keep it in a simple folder and lock down:
- contract clause
- directives/change orders
- site control that day
- crew names
- before/after photos
- equipment logs
- notice deadlines
- witness names
- all messages and photos from phones

If you want the next step, I’d want a version that reads more like a real construction notice, a little colder, with a deadline for their written response.
